title: Data Types: Booleans & Null
description: Represent truth values and the absence of value.

slides.title: Booleans: True or False ✅❌
slides.content:
# Boolean Type

A boolean represents a truth value: either `true` or `false`.

They are often used in conditions (like if statements).

```php
<?php
$isLoggedIn = true;
$isAdmin = false;
?>
```

Case-insensitive: `TRUE`, `False` also work, but lowercase is convention.

slides.title: Null: Nothing at All
slides.content:
# The Null Type

`null` is a special type representing a variable with **no value**.

*   A variable that has been declared but not assigned a value is automatically `null`.
*   You can explicitly set a variable to `null`.
*   Unsetting a variable also makes it `null` in a sense (but better to think of it as undefined).

slides.title: Examples
slides.content:
```php
<?php
$flag = true;
$completed = false;

$result;           // not set, defaults to null
$data = null;      // explicitly null

var_dump($flag);    // bool(true)
var_dump($result);  // NULL
?>
```
slides.codeSnippet:
<?php
$flag = true;
$completed = false;

$result;
$data = null;

var_dump($flag);
var_dump($result);
var_dump($data);
?>

quizData.question: A variable that is declared but not assigned a value has the type `null`.
quizData.explanation: Yes, uninitialized variables are `null` (but PHP will also raise a notice if you try to use them).
